note: The ground floor has a ramped accessible entrance and is largely flat inside, but it's often very crowded and the upper gallery is via a stone ramp/stairs. Main floor manageable; carpeted prayer area.

A ramped accessible entrance exists and the prayer hall floor is flat, but visitor routing, shoe removal and crowds complicate a wheelchair visit; the courtyard has some uneven stone. Partly accessible.

A sprawling palace with cobbled courtyards, thresholds, steps between pavilions and the Harem's stairs; some accessible routes and ramps exist but much is uneven and hilly. Hard for wheels and seniors.
Mostly flat covered lanes but extremely crowded, with worn uneven flooring and thresholds at entrances; navigable in parts but a real squeeze with wheels or a stroller.
Flat single covered hall but very tight and crowded; entrances have small steps or thresholds. Manageable slowly off-peak, hard when busy.
Public and tour ferries have step-free or ramped boarding at major piers and level deck seating; a scenic, relaxed and accessible way to see the city. Confirm the pier is ramped.
A lift reaches the upper viewing levels, but the surrounding Galata streets are steep and cobbled and the very top balcony is tight. Tower has a lift; approach is hilly.
The atmospheric underground cistern is reached by a staircase down; recent renovations added some access but it remains largely stair-based and damp. Hard for wheels.
A long, broad, flat pedestrian avenue that is smooth and step-free end to end (a heritage tram runs down it); easy strolling though very crowded, with hilly side streets.
A hilltop mosque with grand views; ramped access exists to the courtyard and prayer hall but the approach is steep and the terraces uneven. Partly accessible with effort.
A 19th-century waterfront palace with flatter grounds and some accessible routes/ramps on the ground floor, though upper floors and the harem have stairs. Better than the hilltop sites.
